sentences.text: It depends how much time you go.
I'm all right, yeah.
Yeah, and we were, we were sitting in this room.
And we didn't know what's going to happen to us.
So we decided whatever, if they come back again, whatever happens, we're not going down.
What will happen?
If they shoot us, they shoot us.
But we're not going down.
And we made a plan.
We will, the three of us, me, and my sister, and my mother will go up onto the roof, the roof, the main roof of the building.
The roof had a hatch.
And we told my, my brother he should put a padlock onto the hatch from the inside so it should look that it's locked and nobody is up there.
And he will hide himself in a-- we had a big trunk in the room.
Where we-- after this, the uncle and aunt moved out, we had a bit more room, so we, we had a trunk.
And we used to sit on it.
So he should hide himself in this trunk.
And as it happened, after two days they came back again, the same, the gestapo with the Jewish police.
And they started shooting in the air.
And they, they with the [INAUDIBLE] you should come down, and nothing will happen to us.
And we will get food, because they knew that the people that are left are starving.
They stopped all the food rationing, distributing, so this was their bait.
If they will give food, then the people will come.
So we decided we're not going down.
And we put this plan in operation.
We went up onto the loft.
And my brother got into the trunk.
And we heard them coming up.
They ran up the stairs.
They came into the room.
They looked, and they went out again.
And they didn't find us.
And this went on for about 10 days, these deportations.
[NON-ENGLISH] it was called.
And in those 10 days, they deported about 30,000 people.
This was a very terrible-- they called it the nightmare days in the ghetto, these 10 days what happened.
You can imagine, they took away all the children, and all the old people, and a lot of not old people as well.
They-- know that-- you see, why did they take people like us?
Because the police were told that their children are safe.
Their children were not deported.
Not just children, their relatives were also not deported.
So know that the Germans knew exactly how many young people and how many old people there are in the ghetto.
In order to make up the numbers, they took people like us as well.
Anyway, we managed to get over this, which lasted for a few months.
And-- but things changed in the ghetto.
It wasn't the same anymore.
Rumkowski was stripped of a lot of his rights.
And the Germans used to take a more direct interest.
They used to come in more often to the factories to--
I was working, yes.
We were all working.
And the food situation improved a bit after this, after these deportations.
But deportations continued all the time, even after this.
Till finally in 1944, we heard that Poland was already-- half of Poland was occupied by the Russians.
And there was an uprising.
And there was-- about the Warsaw ghetto, which was in 1943, I think, was the Warsaw ghetto uprising.
We heard all about this.
So there were one or two underground radios in the, in the ghetto.
I think they caught these people afterwards and they deported them, the people that operated these radios, because there were a lot of informers, Jewish and German informers.
They used to come into the ghetto and speak Yiddish like Jews.
And they were informers.
They used to go around and, you know, listen to all the gossip that was going on.
Although it wasn't allowed, but this-- you know, you can't stop gossip.
So this finally came to-- we heard that the Germans are being defeated on the-- the Russian Front.
And the Allies have, have landed in France.
An we knew what's going on.
But unfortunately we realized that until we are liberated it will be too late.
We were hoping that something will, a miracle will happen and we would be liberated.
But it wasn't to be.
And this Rumkowski, at the time, towards the end, there were still 70,000 Jews in the ghetto.
They were not from Lodz.
Most of them were from people that were brought in from other places.
But there were still 70,000 Jews.
And maybe he was hoping that he was saved.
You see, if he would have been lucky, let's say, and if these 70,000 Jews would have been saved, he would have been a hero, I suppose, after the war.
So we don't know.
It's very hard to say what was in his mind.
Anyway, it was in the summer of 1944, this-- this German leader, Biebow his name was, the German administrator, gave a speech.
And also in this fish mark.
Yes.
And he, and he said, the front line is coming here.
And the ghetto has to be evacuated.
And we should not worry.
Nothing will happen to us.
All the factories will be transferred into Germany, and we would be much better off there.
And we will be treated much better.
And there'll be no shortage of food.
And we are, we are very important people.
We are a big help to the war effort for the German Reich.
And he said-- he used his face-- not a hair will be missing from your head.
He makes sure that nothing happens to us.
And he's looked after us all these years.
He was responsible, and he told us then that we are the only Jews that are still alive-- he didn't say alive.
He said that are still free.
He called us free in the, in ghettos, in the ghetto, he said.
And he's transferring us to Germany.
And he's making a plan that every day a different factory will be transferred with the families.
He doesn't want to break up the families.
So they should report with their families, and they will be allotted on the carriages special places with families together.
We didn't believe him.
I was-- 1944, I was 17.
So I, we did not believe him.
And we did not report.
When it came to our, our factory was supposed to report, my, my mother, my brothers, we didn't report.
We stayed at home.
But see, the trouble was we had nothing to eat.
See?
The rations were taken away, right away.
Leave?
How could you leave?
There were guards all over the place.
Whoever came near the, the fence was shot on the spot.
Nobody ran away from the ghetto, not as far as I know.
In all the years-- maybe in the beginning, maybe before it was well organized, but afterwards no way.
Because the Jewish police, they-- they kept you away from the fence.
Even if you managed to do a deal with a German guard or something, the Jewish police was in the way.
See, they were-- they--, how shall I say?
Very determined to see that nobody escapes because I think he had to report-- if there was one or two people missing, he had to produce either the body or the person.
If a-- every day people died.
He had to produce the bodies.
See, there was a check up, how many bodies the, the, they buried in the-- in the cemetery.
Nobody could escape from the ghetto.
Anyway, so they started then picking-- if the people didn't report, so they started picking the people from the, from the, from the houses, from the apartments.
So we lived in-- the ghetto, I didn't mention this, was divided into three parts.
Pardon?
--picture later.
The ghetto was this divided into three parts.
And every part, in order to cross from one part into the other, you had to cross over was a bridge.
So what they done is, in order to isolate the part where we lived, they gave an order that whoever is found tomorrow in this part will be shot on sight.
So we hid for a day or two, but it was getting too risky because there weren't many people.
And they were deporting all the time.

So we crossed over into the other part of the ghetto where my-- my uncle wasn't alive anymore-- where his children lived in that room where originally we came in the beginning when we were taken out from our apartment.
So we came in to this room.
And we stayed-- there were four of them because the father was, the father was dead so there were four of them, and us four-- eight people were staying in that room.
And we didn't know what's going to happen to us.
We had no food.
All we had was ersatz coffee, coffee substitute, that's a chicory that we had to eat, nothing else.
And water-- when, if we needed water, we had to go out to get water from a pump.
So it was very risky to go out because they could pick up the people as soon as they saw somebody walking.
Anyway, we stayed in this room for a couple of days till finally we heard that they surrounded this block.
And they're going from room to room to get the people out.
So there was a pit in the room, where they used to keep coal, a coal pit.
So we all went down into that pit and we covered-- it was covered over with, with the floorboards.
And the, we pushed, we managed to push the beds.
There were two beds in that room.
So we pushed the beds over the, the, the pit.
And we heard them come into the room.
And they went around, and they looked for us.
And one said to the other-- they saw our rucksacks laying there-- so one said to the other, they must be here somewhere because their rucksacks are here.
And they looked and looked, but they didn't find us.
So then they went away.
The next day, so, it was a bit quiet.
So we decided instead of sitting all of us in that room, there were plenty of empty rooms because the people, people were taken away.
So we decided to go over to another room.
And we will stay in that other room where it was more space to move about.
But we ran out of water.
So my brother went out to get some water, to a pump.
And they caught him.
So he wouldn't tell them where he came from till they beat him up.
So finally they came back with him.
And they caught us, the four of us.
I mean the three of us and him.
And they, they didn't beat us or anything.
They took us out, and they took us to a tram, which wasn't far away.
And they put us on this tram.
And we went to the-- and we drove down to the railways, where the railway  was to be deported.
And when we came to the railway, they-- each one of us got a loaf of bread, like they promised.
They kept their word.
And they put us on the carriages.
And we waited in this train, felt the train started moving about six, seven hours.
And they packed in about 100 people in the carriage.
There was no room.
We got tired of standing, so one sat on top of the other.
And there were no toilet facilities, so-- so you can imagine the stench what went on there.
Anyway, in the evening we started moving.
We drove the whole night.
In the morning we stopped, came to a place.
We stopped and we managed to look out of the-- through the cracks in the-- they sealed up everywhere these carriages.
We shouldn't see too much.
And one of the people managed to look out and saw somebody with a, walking around with a pair, like a pair of pajamas.
We didn't know anything about it, what this, what this place is.
What it's-- why they are wearing these pajamas.
We stayed there like this in the train, in the wagon, for about two hours.
And all of a sudden we heard noises coming with dogs, and shouting, and screaming.
The doors opened, and they started shouting.
They started hitting us with the rifle butts.
Aus, aus, aus, schnell, schnell, schnell.
And the dogs were barking, and the screaming.
Anyway it was such a mix up, we ran-- we, we fell off these wagons.
And then they, they, they, they separated men one side, women the other side.
My mother and my sister were pushed aside.
And the last words my mother said to me was look after Yitzhak.
That's my younger brother.
And that's it.
And we were pushed along with a crowd of people till somebody came up to me, and he said in Yiddish, he said, if he asks how old you are, you should say you are 18.
And my brother asked this man, where are we?
So he said, you are in Auschwitz, he said.
We didn't know what that word means, Auschwitz.
So he said to-- my brother asked him, what's so special about Auschwitz?
So he said, you'll find out.
And that's it.
And we were pushed along till finally we came to a place, and they pushed us into a barrack.
And then they took us out from this barrack, and we were put in-- there was this assessment standing there.
And rechts, links, right, left, and I was-- I made myself look nice and straight, and my brother as well.
We managed to get through to the right, the side that the people are staying, were staying alive.
Most of the people went to the other side.
We realized then what's happening.
And from there we were taken into a barrack.
Well, we could see the stench from the, the crematorium, the stench of the-- the smell and the-- it was like hell on Earth.
If you can imagine hell on this, on this, on this world, that's how it was.
It, it was-- I can't describe it.
It's impossible to describe it, this, this, what went on there.
And-- and children-- well, there weren't many children because the children they took away before.
But the few children that were left from the police and the other, they were screaming.
And old people, whoever was still there, the people that couldn't get-- the weak people that couldn't get off the train, the train, there were lorries waiting for them.
And they took them away.
I heard later that these people were given a bullet in the head and thrown in the pit.
And burned right away.
So I was told.
And the people that could walk, we were made to walk along a-- a ramp.
And this chap was standing there and was separating us.
As I told you before, we were driven into a barrack.
And we came into the barrack, was chloride up to the knees.
And we were told to get into this barrack.
It was, it was burning the eyes.
They used such a lot of chloride.
We were in this barrack till  it got dark.
In the middle of the night, SS men came in.
And they went around with a very strong torch, and they lit in the people's faces.
And they announced whoever has foreign currency, or, or diamonds, or jewelry should come forward and should hand it over.
Otherwise, the person, every person will be x-rayed.
And if they found-- because we couldn't have anything on us.
It's only what was in the body, because-- we were told to undress.
Anyway, so the people that came forward, they, they, we heard them taken out-- we, we, we saw them being taken out.
And in the dark we couldn't see very much because if they took away the torch it was dark.
And they kept coming back and taking people out, and we heard shots, and shots, and shots to frighten us.
The people should hand over the valuables.
This went on all night like that.
In the morning, we were pushed off into the-- in front of every barrack was such a space where we-- we stood for appell.
In the morning it was bitter cold.
We only had the-- I'm mixing it up a bit.
We were taken into a place before, and we were told to undress.
And we were given these pajamas with a cap, a striped cap to put on.
And from there we were taken out and taken into this place.
And in the morning we, we, we were-- and in all this time we didn't get anything to eat.
So in, in, about lunchtime that day-- in the morning was very cold.
In the daytime it was turning very hot.
So they started distributing some soup.
So whoever queued up for the soup got such a beating that the bowl of soup was more on the floor than they managed to get to eat.
Anyway, then we were picked out three-- 800 boys from all the grown-ups, all the adults.
They separated 800 boys into a separate barrack.
And in this barrack we were for about three days.
Then they  were-- we were told to stand to attention.
And a lot of SS came along, and they picked out 131 boys.
And I was lucky to be-- well my brother was picked out, but I , I ran across.
And they were picked out, they were standing about 10 yards from, from us, these boys that were picked out to work.
We guessed they were taken to work because they picked the healthier boys.
So I just took a chance and I ran across as they were standing there with the dogs and they were busy looking and talking to each other.
I took-- I had nothing to lose.
I just-- you, you learn by instinct.
In such situations, it's a split-second decision you have to make.
And I ran across.
If I will be caught, they would have shot me.
And I managed to get across.
And I was together with my brother.
And then we were taken to a place, into another barrack, where we were given tattoos on the arms.
You see, if you managed to get that tattoo in-- in Auschwitz you were safe for, for the time being you were safe.
Without the tattoo, you were destined to, to the crematorium.
If you had that tattoo, that meant that you were going to be sent to work.
Didn't know how long he would be able to, to, to survive at work.
But at least you weren't taken right away to the crematorium.
So we were given the, the tattoos.
And then we were taken out from this camp.
And we were taken into another camp, where we were put in quarantine for three weeks.
And this camp was-- they had-- it was a show camp because we had proper bunks.
And there were showers in the, in the block.
And this was used-- when the Red Cross used to send their commission, they were shown this camp.
And we were three, we were three weeks in this camp.
And the food was not bad either.
After the three weeks, guards came and took us away from there.
And we were taken to a place called Budy.
No, I've never seen a Red Cross commission.
But apparently they used to come.
I don't know how often, but this camp was used for Red Cross.
There were proper blocks, brick-built blocks, not like the other prefab blocks.
Anyway, we were taken to a place, which was called Budy, that was a farm that supplied food to the main camp.
And we, I was allotted with the other 26-- from the 131 boys, they divided us into three or four groups.
And 26 of us were allotted to this camp.
And we looked after the horses.
We had to make sure-- we had to get up very early in the morning to feed the horses, to clean the horses, and in the evening as well, when they came back.
Some of them were riding horses for the SS.
And some of them, they were working horses.
And in the evening we had to feed and clean the horses again.
So it was a very long day.
By the time we finished with the horses was about 9 o'clock at night.
And we just managed to lay down for a few hours.
And in the morning, the same thing all over again.
That went on for seven days a week, because horses don't know Saturday or Sunday.
You have to work with them all the time.
After about three months being there, I managed, I was lucky, to be allotted to look after sheep.
We were all the time together.
We managed to stick together.
And we were allotted to look after sheep.
The sheep was a lot easier because for the winter-- the winter was coming near.
So they brought the sheep in from the fields.
And they were looked after in such barracks, stables.
So we were indoors.
And first of all it wasn't so cold.
And sheep is a lot easier to look after than horses.
And also after about a month before Auschwitz was liquidated, I was lucky, as I knew, I had experience in tailoring.
I was lucky to be allotted to help a tailor that was working for the Gestapo in-- to repair and make their uniforms.
So he had this workshop outside the camp in the guards quarters.
So he used to take me with a guard-- him, and me, and the guard used to take us every morning to work in the, in this workshop.
So it was a lot easier, and the food was better there as well.
So that's how I managed to be, to stay alive.
And also I managed to help my brother a bit as well.
I used to bring back a bit of food.
And I used to give it to him.
So it's, we helped each other.
I suppose the fact that were together helped, we helped each other.
And that's how we managed to survive.
Maybe without him I wouldn't have survived.
And this went on till finally one day-- I'm missing out a few things-- finally one day in January 1945, we were standing in the morning for the appell.
Every morning and every evening there  was appell, where they counted the people, the prisoners.
And we were told we're not going back to the camp.
To, to, we're not going to work anymore.
The camp is being evacuated.
Just like that, without--
